summary:
Domestic cattle (Bos taurus) descended from the wild aurochs (Bos primigenius), a large and formidable bovine that once ranged across Eurasia and North Africa. Genetic and archaeological evidence indicates that cattle were domesticated at least twice — once in the Near East around 8000–7000 BCE and once independently in Africa. The Near Eastern domestication event is the better documented, with aurochs bones showing morphological changes consistent with captive breeding appearing at sites across Anatolia and the Levant.

Cattle domestication was a major development in early agricultural economies, providing draft power, dairy products, meat, and hides. The ability to use cattle as plow animals substantially increased the productivity of farming, while dairying opened a new and renewable food source. Cattle also became central to the ritual and symbolic worlds of many early agricultural societies, as evidenced by the prominence of bull imagery at sites such as [[event:11|Catalhoyuk]] in Anatolia and in Predynastic Egyptian culture.
